climate_overview: Kamakura has a mild coastal climate on Sagami Bay, south of Yokohama. The Pacific Ocean moderates temperatures, making winters warmer than Tokyo and summers cooled by sea breezes. Annual rainfall of around 1,600mm is well-distributed, with June's rainy season the wettest month. Cherry blossoms bloom slightly earlier than Tokyo due to the coastal warmth. The hydrangea season in June is particularly famous — Kamakura draws enormous crowds for its temple hydrangea displays. Autumn foliage at the wooded temples is excellent. The beach resort at Yuigahama and Zaimokuza is popular from July to September.

january.description: January brings crisp, clear days with ocean breezes that make temperatures feel colder but provide beautiful visibility. The temples and coastal paths are nearly deserted, offering rare solitude at Hasedera, Tsurugaoka Hachimangu, and the Great Buddha. Snow is very rare here due to the coastal location. New Year period sees local visitors at Tsurugaoka Hachimangu for Hatsumode — one of the most attended shrines in the Kanto region, receiving millions in the first three days of January. Plum blossoms appear at Tokei-ji and Zuisenji by late January.
events: Plum blossom season begins at Tsurugaoka

february.description: February is ideal for plum blossom viewing in Kamakura, with Tokei-ji, Zuisenji, and Tsurugaoka Hachimangu putting on excellent displays. The atmosphere at the temples is contemplative and uncrowded. Clear winter days offer exceptional Pacific Ocean views from the hilltop temples and the nearby Yokoyama Hiking Trail. Setsubun bean-throwing ceremonies at Tsurugaoka Hachimangu draw local crowds on February 3. Budget travellers will find this the best value month of the year.

march.description: March brings warming temperatures and early cherry blossoms to Kamakura, which blooms slightly ahead of Tokyo due to its coastal warmth. The coastal hiking trails through cedar and bamboo forests are beautiful in early spring light. Rainfall increases this month and weather is variable. The atmospheric cedar-lined approach to Tsurugaoka Hachimangu is particularly beautiful when trees are greening. Crowds begin building as cherry blossom forecasts are released nationally. Weekend trains from Tokyo become crowded.

april.description: April is peak cherry blossom season in Kamakura, with the Dankazura cherry blossom promenade leading to Tsurugaoka Hachimangu creating one of the most photographed scenes in the Kanto region. The Great Buddha framed by cherry petals is iconic. Engaku-ji's grounds are particularly beautiful. Kamakura becomes very crowded at weekends — consider visiting on a weekday. The narrow streets of Komachi-dori fill with tourists and food stalls. The Kamakura Matsuri festival in the third week of April includes yabusame (horseback archery) demonstrations.

may.description: May brings warm, comfortable weather ideal for hiking Kamakura's wooded temple trails. Golden Week sees enormous visitor numbers from Tokyo (50 minutes by train). Mid-to-late May is excellent after the holiday crowds dissipate — fresh green foliage, comfortable temperatures, and the approach of iris and hydrangea season. The iris gardens at Meigetsuin begin their late-May preparations for June blooming. Surfing and swimming conditions begin to build at Yuigahama beach. The views from Hasedera over the rooftops to the sea are exceptional.

june.description: June is Kamakura's hydrangea season — one of the most famous in Japan, drawing enormous crowds despite the rain. Meigetsuin Temple's hydrangeas are world-famous, with queues forming before opening. Hasedera Temple's hillside hydrangea path offers ocean views through blooms. Meigetsu-in, Engaku-ji, and the roadside verges throughout the city burst with purple, white, and pink ajisai. The combination of misty rain and soft-hued flowers is beautiful. Go early, go on weekdays, and bring waterproof footwear.

july.description: July sees Kamakura transform into a beach resort as Yuigahama and Zaimokuza beaches open for swimming. Surf schools set up, beach huts serve cold drinks and grilled corn, and the normally cultural-tourism town gains a summery party atmosphere. The temples are best visited early morning before heat builds. August fireworks over the coast are the month's highlight. The sea breeze makes Kamakura noticeably more pleasant in summer heat compared to inland cities.

august.description: August is peak beach season at Kamakura, combining cultural sightseeing with coastal resort atmosphere. The Kamakura fireworks festival over the bay is spectacular. Obon festivals in mid-August bring quiet periods to the beaches as local families travel. The Great Buddha at sunset in August haze is a classic summer image. Mornings are the only comfortable time for temple hiking trails. The Hasedera's Bon Odori dancing under lantern light is atmospheric and welcoming to visitors.

september.description: September brings Kamakura's heaviest rainfall month and typhoon season, though the coastal location means weather can be more dramatic but also clearer between storms. Beach season winds down and the resort atmosphere fades as schools restart. The Tsurugaoka Hachimangu holds its Grand Festival in September, including yabusame horseback archery on September 16 — a spectacular display of mounted archery along a 250-metre sand track. This is one of the most dramatic traditional events in the Kanto region.

october.description: October brings excellent conditions to Kamakura — comfortable temperatures, thinning crowds after summer, and the approach of autumn foliage. The coastal forest paths are beautiful in early autumn. Day trips from Tokyo are popular but very manageable compared to peak season. The ocean views from hillside temples take on a crystal-clear quality on fine October days. Autumn colour begins appearing in the wooded temple grounds by month's end. Soba noodle restaurants and traditional tea houses are at their most atmospheric in autumn.

november.description: November brings stunning autumn foliage to Kamakura's wooded temple grounds. Engaku-ji, Tokei-ji, and Jochi-ji in the Kita-Kamakura area are particularly magnificent with maple reds against ancient wooden temple structures. The approach from Kita-Kamakura station through cedar forests in autumn colour is unforgettable. Crowds are significant on weekends but much more manageable than April. Early morning visits on weekdays offer a near-magical experience. The compact size of Kamakura makes it ideal for full-day autumn exploration.
activities: Peak autumn foliage at Kita-Kamakura temples

december.description: December brings quiet, clear days to Kamakura. Late foliage lingers at some temple sites into early month. Winter illuminations are modest compared to the city, but the absence of crowds allows deep engagement with the Great Buddha, the hilltop coastal views, and the forest temple paths. The winter sea is dramatic and beautiful along the coast. New Year's preparations at Tsurugaoka Hachimangu begin in late December, culminating in midnight first-prayers on New Year's Eve. A relaxed and authentic winter escape from Tokyo just 50 minutes away.
events: New Year Eve at Tsurugaoka Hachimangu
